title: PowerShell HTTP Retry And Rate-Limit Handling - Exponential Backoff And Circuit Breaker
applies_to: PowerShell scripts calling Microsoft Graph, Qualys, BitSight, or SharePoint APIs
Handling 429 throttling and transient 5xx errors in Invoke-RestMethod calls
Building resilient API wrappers with retry and circuit-breaker logic

confirmed_facts.fact: Core retry wrapper pattern for Invoke-RestMethod in PowerShell: wrap in a do-while loop with try/catch. Track attempt count and success flag. On 429 or 5xx, extract Retry-After header or apply exponential backoff, then continue. On success or non-retryable error, exit the loop.

Accessing the response status code and headers in a caught exception (PowerShell 7+): `$statusCode = $_.Exception.Response.StatusCode.Value__; $retryAfterHeader = $_.Exception.Response.Headers.GetValues('Retry-After') | Select-Object -First 1`. In PowerShell 5.1, use `-ResponseHeadersVariable` on successful calls and inspect the variable.

Exponential backoff with jitter: `$delay = [Math]::Min([Math]::Pow(2, $attempt) * $baseDelaySecs, $maxDelaySecs) + (Get-Random -Minimum 0.0 -Maximum 1.0)`. Recommended values: `$baseDelaySecs = 2`, `$maxDelaySecs = 64`, `$maxAttempts = 5`. Jitter prevents synchronized retries when multiple script instances are running.

Retryable vs non-retryable status codes: retry on 429 (Too Many Requests), 500 (Internal Server Error), 502 (Bad Gateway), 503 (Service Unavailable), 504 (Gateway Timeout). Do not retry on 400 (Bad Request), 401 (Unauthorized — refresh token once and retry), 403 (Forbidden — do not retry), 404 (Not Found — do not retry). Retrying 4xx errors other than 429 and 401 wastes quota.

Microsoft Graph throttling: returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header (value in seconds). Always honor the header value rather than applying a fixed backoff. Graph SDK cmdlets (Invoke-MgGraphRequest) handle throttling automatically with built-in retry; this is a reason to prefer SDK cmdlets over raw Invoke-RestMethod for Graph calls.

SharePoint Online throttling: returns HTTP 429 or 503 with a Retry-After header. PnP.PowerShell cmdlets automatically handle throttling with built-in retry. For raw REST calls to SharePoint, implement the same explicit retry pattern as for Graph.

Qualys throttling: returns HTTP 429 with no guaranteed Retry-After header. Default backoff strategy: 60 seconds on first 429, then exponential. Qualys also returns 503 during maintenance windows; retry up to 3 times with 30-second intervals before escalating.

BitSight throttling: returns HTTP 429 when the hourly rate limit (~15,000 requests/hour) is exceeded. Honor the Retry-After header if present. For portfolio-wide pulls, add a 250ms sleep between company detail calls (`Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 250`) to stay well within the rate limit.

Circuit-breaker pattern: track consecutive failures in a script-level counter. If failures exceed a threshold (e.g., 5 consecutive), set a `$circuitOpen = $true` flag and stop retrying for a cooldown period (e.g., 300 seconds). Log a warning and return a structured error result rather than crashing. Resume after cooldown. This prevents runaway loops that exhaust API rate limits.

implementation_notes: Encapsulate all retry logic in a single `Invoke-ApiRequest` function that accepts URI, method, headers, and optional body. All API calls throughout the script should go through this function. This keeps retry logic centralized and testable.
For 401 responses, refresh the access token once and retry before treating it as a non-retryable error. Add a `$tokenRefreshed` flag to avoid infinite refresh loops.
caveats: PowerShell 5.1 does not expose response headers on caught exceptions. Use a try/catch that calls Invoke-RestMethod with -ResponseHeadersVariable on the success path, and implement a fixed exponential backoff on the error path.
Start-Sleep blocks the thread. For high-concurrency scripts using PowerShell jobs or runspaces, prefer async sleep patterns to avoid blocking all threads during backoff.
Do not retry within a PnP.PowerShell or Microsoft.Graph SDK command that already has built-in retry. Double-retrying can result in duplicate writes or unexpected state.
